10.3390/jcm14041321.title: Computer-Assisted Protocol-Adherent Blood Lipid Evaluation in Vascular Outpatients (CAPABLE-Vascular)
10.3390/jcm14041321.summary:
We built a rule-based system to automatically order blood lipid and HbA1c tests before vascular surgery outpatient appointments, and found it increased test ordering from under 40% to 100% of eligible patients.

I helped with the automation behind this.

10.1016/j.apjo.2025.100146.title: Medical registries: Factors impacting clinician engagement
10.1016/j.apjo.2025.100146.summary: We explored factors that impact clinician engagement with medical registries, identifying time burden, lack of workflow integration, and limited perceived value as key barriers to sustained participation.

10.1007/s11739-024-03689-2.title: Translational artificial intelligence-led optimization and realization of estimated discharge with a supportive weekend interprofessional flow team (TAILORED-SWIFT)
10.1007/s11739-024-03689-2.summary: We paired an AI discharge-prediction algorithm with a weekend interprofessional flow team, and found a significant increase in weekend discharges (18% vs 14%) with associated cost savings.

10.1111/imj.16506.title: Push or pull? Digital notification platform implementation reduces dysglycaemia
10.1111/imj.16506.summary:
We implemented a digital notification platform to push real-time glycaemic alerts to clinicians, and found it significantly reduced inpatient dysglycaemia events.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the notification platform.

10.1101/2025.07.19.25331814.title: Artificial Intelligence Deployment of Conversational Support (AI-DOCS): A patient acceptability study
10.1101/2025.07.19.25331814.summary:
We deployed a conversational AI assistant to support hospital inpatients and assessed acceptability, finding patients were broadly receptive to AI-generated interactions when used alongside clinician oversight.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the conversational AI platform.

10.1111/imj.70172.title: Clindamycin intravenous to oral switch automatic notifications: a pilot study
10.1111/imj.70172.summary:
We built an automated notification system to prompt clinicians when IV clindamycin could be switched to oral, and found it significantly reduced the IV-to-oral ratio and daily IV doses.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the notification system.

10.1016/j.jocn.2025.111144.title: Epilepsy surgery candidate identification with artificial intelligence: An implementation study
10.1016/j.jocn.2025.111144.summary: We implemented a machine learning algorithm to identify epilepsy surgery candidates from clinic data, finding that 53% of AI-flagged patients met criteria for surgery evaluation.

10.1111/imj.16590.title: Copying in medical documentation: developing an evidence‐based approach
10.1111/imj.16590.summary: We quantified erroneous copy-paste in ward round notes, finding 8.3% contained copying errors concentrated in the issues list, and developed similarity metrics to automatically detect at-risk notes.

10.1111/imj.16565.title: Rule‐based clinician‐developed programmes can facilitate haemodialysis clinical workflows
10.1111/imj.16565.summary:
We built an automated haemodialysis patient finder to detect maintenance dialysis patients admitted under non-nephrology services, achieving zero false positives and zero false negatives over six months.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the patient detection system.

10.1016/j.jocn.2024.110847.title: Large language models can effectively extract stroke and reperfusion audit data from medical free-text discharge summaries
10.1016/j.jocn.2024.110847.summary: We used a locally-deployed LLM to extract stroke audit data from free-text discharge summaries, achieving 93.8% accuracy across binary, categorical, datetime, and free-text fields.

10.1093/cid/ciae651.title: A Generalizable Risk Factor: Socioeconomic Status and Multiresistant Organism Colonization
10.1093/cid/ciae651.summary: We studied ~50,000 hospital admissions in South Australia and found that lower socioeconomic status was significantly associated with higher multiresistant organism colonisation prevalence, confirming generalisability of international findings.

10.1111/ans.19383.title: The Adelaide Score: prospective implementation of an artificial intelligence system to improve hospital and cost efficiency
10.1111/ans.19383.summary:
We prospectively implemented the Adelaide Score AI system to predict discharge readiness, finding it reduced median length of stay by 0.2 days and seven-day readmission rates, saving an estimated $9.5M annually.

I maintain the inference server for this.

10.1093/ijpp/riae064.title: Medication shortage behaviour change with multidisciplinary clinician-designed digital notification intervention
10.1093/ijpp/riae064.summary: We deployed a clinician-designed digital notification system during an IV paracetamol shortage, and found it significantly reduced unnecessary intravenous paracetamol use.

10.1111/ajr.70037.title: When One Size Does not Fit All—Artificial Intelligence in Australian Rural Health
10.1111/ajr.70037.summary: We examined the challenges of deploying AI in rural Australian hospitals, finding that connectivity limitations, smaller patient populations, and workforce constraints require tailored approaches beyond metropolitan AI models.

10.1016/j.idh.2025.05.001.title: Zero-shot large language model application for surgical site infection auditing
10.1016/j.idh.2025.05.001.summary:
We applied a zero-shot LLM to identify surgical site infections from clinical notes, finding it could accurately flag cases without any task-specific training data or fine-tuning.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the auditing pipeline.

10.1016/j.hrthm.2025.03.570.title: PO-02-088 THE IMPACT OF POSTOPERATIVE ATRIAL FIBRILLATION AFTER CARDIAC SURGERY
10.1016/j.hrthm.2025.03.570.summary: We studied 1,254 cardiac surgery patients and found that postoperative atrial fibrillation extended ICU stay by 0.6 days and post-ICU stay by 1.6 days, with doubled long-term mortality risk.

10.1038/s41433-025-03767-5.title: LLM-assisted medical documentation: efficacy, errors, and ethical considerations in ophthalmology
10.1038/s41433-025-03767-5.summary:
We evaluated LLM-assisted autocomplete for ophthalmology clinic documentation, characterising error types and ethical considerations of integrating AI into clinical note-writing workflows.

I built the clinical text editor and LLM integration behind this.

10.1002/bco2.299.title: Suprapubic catheter change: Evaluating YouTube videos as a resource for teaching junior doctors
10.1002/bco2.299.summary: We evaluated YouTube videos as a teaching resource for suprapubic catheter changes, finding variable quality and identifying criteria for selecting appropriate educational content for junior doctors.

10.1186/s12886-025-04193-7.title: Zero-shot LLM-based visual acuity extraction: a pilot study
10.1186/s12886-025-04193-7.summary:
We used a zero-shot LLM to extract visual acuity measurements from ophthalmology clinical notes, demonstrating feasibility of automated structured data extraction without task-specific training.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the extraction pipeline.

10.1111/imj.70072.title: Large language models for infectious diseases require evidence generation and regulation
10.1111/imj.70072.summary: We outlined the evidence generation, regulatory, and guideline requirements for deploying LLMs in infectious diseases, proposing retrieval-augmented generation as a strategy to minimise hallucination risk.

10.1016/j.hlc.2025.06.609.title: Comparative Performance of Large Language Models vs Clinicians in Creating Cardiology Ward Round Notes: Promises, Limitations, and Implications for Clinical Practice
10.1016/j.hlc.2025.06.609.summary: We compared LLM-generated cardiology ward round notes to clinician notes, finding LLMs produced more complete documentation but occasionally included commission errors and vague recommendations.

10.1177/18333583251377892.title: Automating cancer registries: Pearls and pitfalls
10.1177/18333583251377892.summary:
We explored automating the Australian Brain Cancer Registry using AI-driven data extraction, identifying both the potential efficiency gains and practical challenges of deploying LLMs for registry data collection.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the registry platform.

10.1071/ah25085.title: The influence of specialty training selection criteria on medical students’ career pathway choices in Australia
10.1071/ah25085.summary:
We investigated how specialty training selection criteria influence medical students' career pathway choices in Australia, identifying which criteria most strongly shape early career decisions.

I built the analysis and visualisation tooling.

10.2196/67321.title: Predicting the Emergency Department Patient Journey Using a Machine Learning Approach
10.2196/67321.summary: We developed a machine learning model to predict the emergency department patient journey, enabling early identification of patients likely to require admission or extended stays.

10.1136/bmjno-2025-anzan.117.title: 3567 Current specialty training selection criteria reduce medical students’ interest in neurology as a career pathway
10.1136/bmjno-2025-anzan.117.summary:
We surveyed medical students and found that exposure to specialty selection criteria significantly reduced interest across multiple specialties, with neurology, ICU, and ophthalmology most affected. Research requirements and competitiveness were the key deterrents.

I built the analysis and visualisation tooling.

10.2139/ssrn.5698664.title: Electronic Quantification of Bowel Timing and Automated Alerts Reduce Inpatient Constipation
10.2139/ssrn.5698664.summary:
We quantified bowel motion timing electronically and delivered automated daily notifications to physicians when patients exceeded 72 hours without a bowel motion, reducing the rate of severe constipation (BNO ≥5 days) from 5.6% to 1.9%.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the notification platform.

10.1111/tme.70020.title: Intravenous immunoglobulin weaning evaluation with zero‐shot large language model classification
10.1111/tme.70020.summary:
We applied a zero-shot LLM to classify neurology outpatients' suitability for IVIg weaning from clinic notes, achieving 78.6% classification accuracy and identifying a conservatively estimated $84,702 in potential annual cost savings from IVIg de-escalation.

I helped engineer this into production. I maintain the extraction pipeline.
